claim: Post-2010 European fiscal consolidation intensity negatively predicts subsequent output-gap closure speed and cumulative output over the 2010-2019 window, with the effect concentrated in the Eurozone periphery where the combination of sovereign spreads, no monetary- policy-at-country-level, and high initial output gaps produced a multiplier larger than the pre-crisis consensus assumed. The hypothesis is consistent with the Blanchard-Leigh 2013 finding that IMF fiscal-multiplier assumptions were systematically too low in the early austerity period. The pre-registered claim is that in the Eurozone sample 2010-2019, countries with larger cyclically-adjusted primary-balance tightening over 2010-2013 experienced slower output- gap closure relative to the post-2008 trough, holding initial output gap and debt level constant. The core-vs-periphery split is expected to show the periphery effect substantially larger than the core effect.

covers_claims_inbound.claim_text: The Cameron–Osborne 2010–2016 UK austerity produced a slower output recovery than counterfactual balanced or expansionary fiscal paths would have produced, because fiscal consolidation at near-ZLB has large output costs.
covers_claims_inbound.claim_notes: Phase 4C candidate: claim is UK-specific Cameron-Osborne 2010-2016 counterfactual; linked hypothesis is Eurozone-periphery austerity panel 2010-2019 with UK in sample but not focal. Generality gap — either relink to a UK-specific austerity hypothesis or broaden hypothesis scope.

covers_claims_inbound.claim_text: Post-2010 Eurozone austerity under ECB/IMF/EC programmes in Greece, Portugal, Spain, and Ireland produced larger negative output multipliers than pre-crisis IMF models assumed.
covers_claims_inbound.claim_notes: Phase 4C candidate: audit flagged outcome mismatch (claim coded monetary_policy, hypothesis coded employment_labour) but LLM correctly identified identical proposition. Canonical scope recorded; outcome_dim now aligned on gdp_growth/fiscal_policy/employment_labour to match hypothesis framing.

covers_claims_inbound.claim_text: Post-2010 Eurozone austerity in Greece, Spain, Portugal, Ireland produced larger-than-projected output contractions, falsifying the R&R 90%-debt-threshold austerity case.

Trump's 2017 TCJA produced smaller investment and output responses than Laffer-curve advocates projected, consistent with New Keynesian estimates of corporate-tax-cut passthrough in a near-full-employment economy.
covers_claims_inbound.claim_notes: NK predicts positive but modest effects, not revenue-neutrality. Phase 4C candidate: claim tests Trump 2017 TCJA; linked hypothesis tests Reagan 1981-86 tax cuts. Era and entity mismatch — needs new tcja_2017_growth_effect hypothesis.

claim: Progressive income-tax marginal rates (up to roughly 70% top rate) have been compatible with strong growth in post-war US 1945-1980 and Nordics, falsifying extreme-Laffer-curve positions.

covers_claims_inbound.claim_text: Progressive income-tax marginal rates (up to roughly 70% top rate) have been compatible with strong growth in post-war US 1945-1980 and Nordics, falsifying extreme-Laffer-curve positions.

claim: UK GDP per capita (PPP, constant international dollars) diverged negatively from a matched synthetic counterfactual of similar-income anglophone/developed economies (USA, CAN, AUS, NZL, DEU, NLD, CHE) starting around 2008 and widening post-2016 (Brexit referendum). The cumulative divergence by 2023 is meaningfully large and not explained by measurement differences or demographic composition. The policy- content fingerprint of UK decline is a combination of planning- restriction supply constraint + pro-cyclical fiscal stance 2001–2010 + austerity 2010–2016 + Brexit trade friction + industrial-energy-cost regime; no single movement is the decline, but the cumulative effect is identifiable.

covers_claims_inbound.claim_text: UK labour share fell following Thatcherite reforms 1979-1990 (council-housing privatisation, union-law tightening, financial deregulation) by a magnitude not explained by sectoral composition shifts alone.
covers_claims_inbound.claim_notes: Phase 4C candidate — FAIL vs current hypothesis (linked hyp tests UK 1996-2023 divergence centred on Brexit/austerity; this claim is Thatcher 1979-1990 labour-share). Candidate for new hypothesis `uk_thatcher_era_labour_share_decline_1979_1990` or relink to a labour-share-decline hypothesis.
